title: Non-conservative H^{1/2-} weak solutions of the incompressible 3D Euler equations

thm:1.1.summary: MAIN RESULT: For any $\beta \in (0,1/2)$, any $L^2$ divergence-free $v_start, v_end$ with same mean, any T>0 and $\varepsilon>0$, there exists a weak solution v $\in$ C([0,T]; $H^\beta(T^3))$ of 3D Euler with $||v(0)-v_start||_{L^2} \le \varepsilon$ and $||v(T)-v_end||_{L^2} \le \varepsilon$. Establishes flexibility (hence non-uniqueness and non-conservation) in $C^0_t H^{1/2-}_x$.

rem:1.2.summary: Corollaries of the proof: (1) nontrivial weak solutions with compact time support $\Rightarrow$ non-uniqueness in $C^0_t H^{1/2-}_x$; (2) weak solutions attaining any prescribed smooth energy profile e(t), including dissipative solutions.

prop:3.6.summary: Main inductive proposition: given $(v_q$, R̊_q) satisfying the Euler-Reynolds system and all inductive estimates at level q, constructs $(v_{q+1}$, R̊_{q+1}) satisfying the same estimates at level q+1. Parameters $\beta \in [1/3, 1/2)$, b $\in (1, 1/2\beta)$, and a sufficiently large base a_*. The entire proof of thm:1.1 reduces to iterating this proposition.

rem:3.7.summary: Compact time support variant: modify $v_0$ to be a shear flow times a temporal cutoff. The inductive time-support assumption (3.2.1) propagates, yielding $supp_t$ v $\subset [-T,T]$ for a large enough.

rem:3.8.summary: Energy profile variant: the scheme can be modified to attain any smooth energy profile e(t). Requires adjusting the energy iterate through the higher-order stress steps n $\to$ n+1 and p $\to$ p+1. Cutoff difficulty already addressed in BV19.

prop:4.1.summary: Geometric decomposition: for R near Id in symmetric $3\times3$ matrices, R $= \Sigma_{\xi\in\Xi} \gamma_\xi(R)^2 \xi$⊗$\xi$ with $\Xi \subset S^2 \cap Q^3$ and smooth positive amplitude functions $\gamma_\xi$. Each $\xi$ comes with rational orthonormal basis ${\xi, \xi^(2), \xi^(3)}$ and minimal integer n_*. The algebraic foundation for canceling the Reynolds stress.

prop:4.3.summary: Rotating, shifting, periodizing: constructs $T^3-periodic$ pipe-shaped functions $\kappa^k_{\lambda,r,\xi}$ from a compactly-supported profile $\kappa$. Pipes are disjoint for distinct shift indices k, with $T^3/(\lambdar)-periodicity$ and $T^3_\xi/(\lambdarn_*)-periodicity$.

prop:4.4.summary: Intermittent pipe flow construction: builds divergence-free, pressureless Euler solutions $W^k_{\xi,\lambda,r}$ concentrated on pipes of width $~1/\lambda$ spaced at $~1/(\lambdar)$. Key identity: ⟨$W^k$ ⊗ $W^k$⟩ $= \xi$⊗$\xi$. Satisfies $L^p$ estimates $||\nabla^n W||_{L^p}$ ≲ $\lambda^n r^{2/p-1}$. These replace the Beltrami waves of BV19 and are the core building blocks.

lem:4.7.summary: Deformed pipe control: when straight pipes are transported by a Lipschitz flow v, their axes bend by at most $\Gamma_{q+1}^{-1}$, diameters grow by factor $(1+\Gamma_{q+1}^{-1})$, and spacing is preserved up to $\Gamma_{q+1}^{-1}$. Ensures pipe geometry survives the flow deformation.

prop:4.8.summary: Placement lemma: given existing bent pipes in a domain $\Omega$ of diameter $\le 16(\lambda_{q+1}r_1)^{-1}$, finds a new set of straight pipes $W^{k_0}_{\lambda_{q+1},r_2,\xi}$ with disjoint support from all existing pipes, provided $C_* C_A r_2^4 \le r_1^3$. This geometric separation is why the higher-order stress scheme works — each new generation of pipes avoids all previous generations.

lem:5.1.summary: Mollification of the Euler-Reynolds system: smooths $(v_q$, R̊_q) at spatial scale $\lambda$̃$_q^{-1}$ and temporal scale $\tau$̃_{q-1}. Produces mollified pair $(v_{ℓ_q}$, R̊_{ℓ_q}) satisfying E-R system plus commutator stress R̊$_q^{comm}$. Establishes that $u_q = v_{ℓ_q} - v_{$ℓ_{q-1}} satisfies the primary inductive assumption at level q.

prop:7.1.summary: Inductive step on q: given mollified $(v_{ℓ_q}$, R̊_{ℓ_q}) from lem:5.1 and all inductive assumptions, constructs $v_{q+1} = v_{ℓ_q} + w_{q+1}$ and R̊_{q+1} satisfying: (1) Euler-Reynolds system, $(2) w_{q+1}$ bounds with $\Gamma_{q+1}^{-1}$ gain, (3) R̊_{q+1} bounds with $\Gamma_{q+1}^{-C_R}$ gain, (4) time support propagation. Reduces to the three-case induction on ñ (props 7.3–7.5).

prop:7.3.summary: Base case ñ=0 of the inner induction: adds $w_{q+1,0}$ to correct the zeroth-order stress R̊_{ℓ_q}. Produces higher-order stresses H̊_{q,n,p}^0 for $1 \le$ n $\le n_max, 1 \le$ p $\le p_max$, plus remainder R̊_{q+1}^0. Error terms distributed across frequency scales $\lambda_{q,n,p}$.

prop:7.4.summary: Inductive step ñ$-1 \to$ ñ for $1 \le$ ñ $\le n_max-1$: adds $w_{q+1,ñ}$ to correct the ñ-th higher-order stress $\Sigma_p$ H̊_{q,ñ,p}^{n'}, producing new higher-order stresses at levels n > ñ. Same pipe flow construction but at intermediate frequency scale $\lambda_{q$,ñ,p}.

prop:7.5.summary: Final case ñ $= n_max$: adds $w_{q+1,n_max}$ to correct the last higher-order stress, producing only the next-level Reynolds stress R̊_{q+1}. This closes the inner induction and delivers the conclusions of prop:7.1.

lem:8.1.summary: Master amplitude estimate: bounds $||D^N D_{t,q}^M a_{\xi,i,j,k,q,ñ,p̃,l⃗}||_{L^r}$ in terms of $\delta_{q+1$,ñ,p̃}^{1/2}, $\Gamma_{q+1}^{j+2}$, and the frequency/time scales. The amplitudes a encode the stress cutoff geometry and drive the pipe flow perturbation.

lem:8.4.summary: Transport error estimate: $D_{t,q} w_{q+1,ñ} =$ div(H $+ R*)(\partial_t$ w $+ v_{ℓ_q}\cdot\nablaw) + \nablap$ with $||\cdot||_{L^1}$ ≲ $\delta_{q+2} \Gamma_{q+1}^{-C_R-1}$. Uses the inverse divergence (prop:A.18) to absorb the transport error into a controlled Reynolds stress.

lem:8.5.summary:
Nash error estimate: $w_{q+1,ñ}\cdot
ablav_{$ℓ_q} = div(H $+ R*)(w\cdot
ablav) + 
ablap$ with same bound. The Nash error is the 'stretching' term from the nonlinearity; controlled via inverse divergence.

lem:8.6.summary: Type 1 oscillation error estimate: three cases — (1) high-frequency residual at level $(n_max, p_max+1)$ goes into R̊_{q+1} with $\delta_{q+2} \Gamma_{q+1}^{-C_R-1}$ bound; (2) same but local portion; (3) medium-frequency terms go into H̊_{q,n,p} at their natural scale $\delta_{q+1,n,p}$. The oscillation error is the dominant error in convex integration — controlled here via the inverse divergence and Lp decorrelation.

lem:8.8.summary: Overlap counting: the number of cutoff functions $\eta$ whose supports intersect a given $\eta$ is bounded by $C_\eta \Gamma_{q+1}$, with $C_\eta$ depending only on $n_max, p_max, j_max$. Ensures that pipe flow interactions are sparse enough for the $L^2$ estimates to close.

lem:8.12.summary: Type 2 oscillation errors vanish identically. This is the payoff of the disjoint pipe placement (prop:4.8): when two pipe flows from different generations have disjoint support, their cross-interaction in the stress tensor is exactly zero. This is the key structural innovation over BV19.

lem:8.13.summary: Divergence corrector error: the error from making $w_{q+1,ñ}$ exactly divergence-free satisfies $||\cdot||_{L^1}$ ≲ $\Gamma_{q+1}^{-C_R-1} \delta_{q+2}$. Small compared to the inductive stress bound.

lem:A.1.summary: Standard transport estimates: $C^0, C^1, C^N$ bounds for solutions of $\partial_t$ f $+ u\cdot\nablaf =$ g, plus $C^N$ bounds for the flow map $\Phi$ and its derivatives. Classical tool used throughout.

lem:A.2.summary: $L^p$ decorrelation: for a slow function f (derivatives bounded by $\lambda^N)$ and a fast $(T/\mu)^3-periodic$ function g with $\mu/\lambda$ large, $||fg||_{L^p}$ ≲ $C_f ||g||_{L^p}$. Replaces the Fefferman-Stein $L^2$ decorrelation of BV19; this $L^1$ version is essential for the $L^1-based$ stress estimates in the pipe flow scheme.

lem:A.3.summary: Sobolev inequality with cutoffs: upgrades $L^p$ estimates on $\psi_i$ f to $L^\infty$ estimates on $\psi_i^2$ f at the cost of 3/p spatial derivatives. Used to convert the cutoff-localized $L^2$ bounds into the $L^\infty$ estimates needed for the flow map control.

prop:A.17.summary: Inverse divergence iteration step: for a vector field G and a periodic function $\rho = \Delta\theta$ transported by a volume-preserving $\Phi$, decomposes $G^i \rho$∘$\Phi = \partial_n$ R̊$^{in} + \partial_i$ P $+ E^i$ where R̊ is traceless symmetric (supported in supp G) and E involves $\partial(A\cdotA -$ Id) derivatives of $\theta$∘$\Phi$. The one-step building block for the intermittency-friendly inverse divergence.

prop:A.18.summary: Full inverse divergence with error: iterates prop:A.17 d times to decompose G $\rho$∘$\Phi =$ div R̊ $+ \nablaP +$ E where R̊ and P are supported in supp G, ||R̊$||_{L^1}$ ≲ $C_G C_* \zeta^{-1} \Lambda^\alpha$, and the error E has the crucial gain $(\lambda/\zeta)^d \to 0$. When d is large enough, E can be absorbed into a nonlocal stress $R_{nonlocal}$ satisfying $\delta_{q+2}/\lambda_{q+1}^5$ bounds. This is the paper's main technical innovation: an inverse divergence that respects intermittency (compact support) and produces errors small enough for the convex integration to close at regularity $H^{1/2-}$.

edges.note: thm:1.1 is proved by iterating prop:3.6: starting from smooth $(v_0$, R̊_0) satisfying the base case, prop:3.6 propagates inductive estimates to all q $\ge 0$, and the limit v = lim $v_q$ lies in $C^0_t H^{\beta'}_x$ for any $\beta' < \beta$.
The mollification lem:5.1 is applied at each step q to produce $(v_{ℓ_q}$, R̊_{ℓ_q}) from $(v_q$, R̊_q), verifying the primary inductive assumption for $u_q$.
Both corollaries (compact time support, prescribed energy profile) are variants of the same convex integration scheme with modified initial data $v_0$.
Compact time support variant: same scheme but $v_0 = E\varphi(t)(sin x_3, 0, 0)$ with temporal cutoff $\varphi$.
Energy profile variant: requires additional energy tracking through the higher-order stress steps n $\to$ n+1.
prop:3.6 reduces to prop:7.1: given the mollified pair from lem:5.1, prop:7.1 constructs $w_{q+1}$ and R̊_{q+1} satisfying all conclusions.
prop:7.1 reduces to the three-case inner induction: prop:7.3 handles the base case ñ=0 (correcting R̊_{ℓ_q}).
prop:7.4 handles intermediate steps $1 \le$ ñ $\le n_max-1 (correcting$ higher-order stresses).
prop:7.5 handles the final case ñ $= n_max (closing$ the inner induction and delivering R̊_{q+1}).
prop:7.1 takes as input the mollified pair $(v_{ℓ_q}$, R̊_{ℓ_q}) and commutator R̊$_q^{comm}$ from lem:5.1.
The zeroth-order perturbation $w_{q+1,0}$ is built from pipe flows $W^k_{\xi,\lambda,r}$ constructed in prop:4.4, with amplitudes determined by the geometric decomposition.
The amplitude coefficients $a_{\xi}$ use the geometric decomposition R $= \Sigma \gamma_\xi^2 \xi$⊗$\xi$ from prop:4.1 to cancel R̊_{ℓ_q} at leading order.
Transport error from $D_{t,q} w_{q+1,0}$ is bounded by lem:8.4.
Nash error from $w_{q+1,0}\cdot\nablav_{$ℓ_q} is bounded by lem:8.5.
Type 1 oscillation errors from self-interaction of $w_{q+1,0}$ are sorted into higher-order stresses H̊_{q,n,p}^0 by lem:8.6.
Type 2 oscillation errors (cross-interactions with previous-generation pipes) vanish by lem:8.12 due to disjoint support.
Divergence corrector errors bounded by lem:8.13.
Each intermediate step places new pipes avoiding all existing bent pipes via prop:4.8's geometric placement lemma.
Control on deformed pipe geometry (axes, support, spacing) from lem:4.7 provides the hypotheses needed for prop:4.8.
Type 1 oscillation errors at intermediate levels bounded by lem:8.6.
Type 2 oscillation errors vanish by disjoint support (lem:8.12).
Final perturbation $w_{q+1,n_max}$ places pipes at the finest scale, again avoiding all previous pipes via prop:4.8.
Remaining oscillation errors go into R̊_{q+1} via lem:8.6 case (2).
Type 2 errors vanish (lem:8.12).
Transport error is absorbed via the intermittency-friendly inverse divergence prop:A.18.
Nash error is absorbed via the inverse divergence prop:A.18.
Type 1 oscillation error decomposition uses prop:A.18 for both the local (H) and nonlocal (R*) portions.
$L^p$ decorrelation (lem:A.2) separates the slow amplitude from the fast pipe flow in the oscillation error estimates.
Amplitude estimates from lem:8.1 feed into the oscillation error bounds.
Type 2 errors vanish because prop:4.8 guarantees disjoint support of pipe flows from different generations.
Divergence corrector error uses the inverse divergence prop:A.18.
prop:A.18 iterates the single-step inverse divergence prop:A.17 d times to achieve the $(\lambda/\zeta)^d$ gain.
The decorrelation lemma A.2 is used in each iteration step to separate slow from fast components.
Pipe flows $W^k_{\xi,\lambda,r}$ are built from the rotated/shifted/periodized profiles of prop:4.3.
The rational direction vectors $\xi \in \Xi$ and orthonormal frames from prop:4.1 determine the pipe orientations.
Deformed pipe geometry bounds from lem:4.7 establish the hypotheses (axis control, length bounds, neighborhood containment) for the placement argument.
The new pipe flows placed by prop:4.8 are the $W^{k_0}_{\lambda_{q+1},r_2,\xi}$ from prop:4.4.
Transport estimates from lem:A.1 control the commutator stress R̊$_q^{comm}$ arising from mollification.
Sobolev inequality with cutoffs (lem:A.3) upgrades $L^2$ amplitude bounds to $L^\infty$ bounds needed for the flow map estimates.

geometric_foundations.description: Algebraic and geometric infrastructure: matrix decomposition into $\xi$⊗$\xi$ directions, pipe flow construction, periodization, and the placement lemma ensuring disjoint support across generations

mollification.description: Smoothing the Euler-Reynolds system at each inductive step, producing the commutator stress and verifying the primary velocity increment estimate

main_induction_outer.description: The outer induction on q: given level-q data, construct level-(q+1) via the inner three-case induction on ñ

main_induction_inner.description: The inner induction on ñ (higher-order stress levels): base case (ñ=0), intermediate steps $(1 \le$ ñ $\le n_max-1)$, and final case (ñ $= n_max)$. Each step adds a perturbation $w_{q+1,ñ}$ at an intermediate frequency scale.

error_estimates.description: Bounding the four types of error: transport, Nash, Type 1 oscillation (self-interaction sorted into higher-order stresses), Type 2 oscillation (cross-interaction, vanishes by disjoint support), and divergence corrector

inverse_divergence.description: The intermittency-friendly inverse divergence: iterates the single-step decomposition G $\rho$∘$\Phi =$ div R̊ $+ \nablaP +$ E to achieve arbitrary polynomial gain, respecting the compact support of the cutoff. The core technical innovation enabling $H^{1/2-}$ regularity.

analytic_tools.description: Standard tools: transport estimates, $L^p$ decorrelation for $slow\timesfast$ products, Sobolev embedding with cutoffs

main_results_and_variants.description: The main theorem and its corollaries: flexibility of 3D Euler in $C^0_t H^{1/2-}_x$, compact time support variant, prescribed energy profile variant

BV19.title: Nonuniqueness of weak solutions to the Navier-Stokes equation

BDLSV17.title: Onsager's conjecture for admissible weak solutions

DeLellisSzekelyhidi09.title: The Euler equations as a differential inclusion

DaneriSzekelyhidi17.title: Non-uniqueness and h-principle for Hölder-continuous weak solutions of the Euler equations

cheskidov2020sharp.title: Sharp nonuniqueness for the Navier-Stokes equations

BCV18.title: Wild solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations whose singular sets in time have Hausdorff dimension strictly less than 1
